message.content:
You are confirmer #2, independent. Confirm this fix is BOTH appropriate (correct, no regression, idiomatic) AND complete (the named test DISCRIMINATES — fails if reverted). Read the actual changed file + test at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay.
Fix J-001 in scripts/capture_transcripts.py
What: scrubber FAILS CLOSED: decode errors='replace' (always scrub text), classify binary by a NUL byte not UTF-8 validity — fixes the CV-O-1 strict-decode fail-open re-leak.
Test(s): test_scrub_fails_closed_on_invalid_utf8_bytes, test_copy_scrubbed_scrubs_file_with_invalid_utf8
Verdict CONFIRMED or CONCERN; be specific.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	#!/usr/bin/env python3
2	"""Capture the active Claude session's JSONL transcript(s) into the repo for provenance.
3	
4	Run by the pre-commit hook (.githooks/pre-commit) so every commit pulls in an up-to-date
5	snapshot of the agent interactions/effort behind it — an auditable record alongside the work.
6	
7	What it captures: the *active* session (the most-recently-modified top-level transcript in the
8	Claude project dir) plus that session's ``<id>/`` subtree (subagents + workflow transcripts).
9	Other/unrelated sessions are left out. Credential-shaped secrets are redacted on the way in.
10	
11	Honest scope: transcripts still contain local filesystem paths and may contain the operator's
12	email and internal reasoning. If this repo is published, review/exclude ``transcripts/`` first
13	(see transcripts/README.md). The wheel never ships them (only ``src/assay_engine`` is packaged).
14	
15	Config: ASSAY_TRANSCRIPT_SRC overrides the source dir. Exits 0 on any error (never blocks a
16	commit).
17	"""
18	
19	from __future__ import annotations
20	
21	import datetime as _dt
22	import hashlib
23	import json
24	import os
25	import re
26	import sys
27	from pathlib import Path
28	
29	DEFAULT_SRC = Path.home() / ".claude" / "projects" / "-Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc"
30	DEST = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent / "transcripts" / "sessions"
31	MANIFEST = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent / "transcripts" / "MANIFEST.json"
32	
33	# Credential-shaped secrets to redact (defense). Pass 3 (#F-024) extends this to operator PII —
34	# email addresses and the home-directory username embedded in filesystem paths — so committing
35	# transcripts as provenance (the campaign mandate) does not also publish the operator's identity.
36	# Reasoning content and the path STRUCTURE remain (legitimate provenance); see the README caveat
37	# for the full public-release consideration.
38	_SECRET_PATTERNS = [
39	    re.compile(r"sk-ant-[A-Za-z0-9_-]{2,}"),  # any sk-ant- token incl. oat01 OAuth + placeholders
40	    re.compile(r"\bgh[posru]_[A-Za-z0-9]{20,}\b"),  # GitHub tokens
41	    re.compile(r"\bAKIA[0-9A-Z]{16}\b"),  # AWS access key id
42	    # bearer tokens incl. base64url/base64 JWTs — must cover + / = or the token is split at the
43	    # first such char and only the leading fragment is redacted (pass 3, #F-051).
44	    re.compile(r"(?i)bearer\s+[A-Za-z0-9._\-+/=]{20,}"),
45	    # explicit OAuth/token env values: "...OAUTH_TOKEN":"<value>" / ...KEY=<value>
46	    re.compile(r'((?:OAUTH_TOKEN|_TOKEN|_KEY|_SECRET)"\s*:\s*")[^"]{8,}(")'),
47	    re.compile(r"((?:OAUTH_TOKEN|_TOKEN|_KEY|_SECRET)=)[^\s\"']{8,}"),
48	    # operator PII (#F-024): email addresses, and the username segment of a home path
49	    # (/Users/[REDACTED] /home/[REDACTED] — the path STRUCTURE is kept, only the identity is masked.
50	    re.compile(r"\b[A-Za-z0-9._%+-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.-]+\.[A-Za-z]{2,}\b"),  # email → [REDACTED]
51	    re.compile(r"(/(?:Users|home)/)[^/\s\"']+"),  # /Users/[REDACTED] → /Users/[REDACTED]    # The Claude project-dir SLUG (e.g. -Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc): redact the username segment
53	    # regardless of length (#H-018) — the bare-token rule below skips short handles to avoid
54	    # over-redacting common words, but the slug form is unambiguous PII at any length.
55	    re.compile(r"(-(?:Users|home)-)[^-\s/\"']+"),  # -Users-[REDACTED]- → -Users-[REDACTED]-
56	]
57	
58	
59	def _username_pattern() -> re.Pattern[str] | None:
60	    """A redaction for the bare operator username wherever it appears (#G-007).
61	
62	    The /Users|/home path rule only masks the username inside a path; the same handle leaks via
63	    git author strings, prose, and the Claude project-dir slug (``-Users-[REDACTED]-...``). Derive the
64	    handle from the home directory and redact it as a standalone token too. Skipped for trivially
65	    short/None handles (e.g. a CI 'root') to avoid over-redacting common words.
66	    """
67	    try:
68	        name = Path.home().name
69	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 - never break capture over a missing home
70	        return None
71	    if not name or len(name) < 4:
72	        return None
73	    return re.compile(re.escape(name))
74	
75	
76	def _display_name_patterns() -> list[re.Pattern[str]]:
77	    """Redactions for the operator's full DISPLAY NAME (#J-002).
78	
79	    The username/email rules leave the real name (e.g. a git ``user.name`` like "[REDACTED]")
80	    in cleartext — distinct operator PII that appears in git author strings, ``pyproject``
81	    ``authors=``, and prose, often right beside an already-redacted email. Derive candidate names
82	    from ``git config user.name`` and the ``ASSAY_SCRUB_NAMES`` env (comma-separated), redact each
83	    (and, for a multi-word name, its whitespace-flexible form). Length-guarded to avoid
84	    over-redacting common words.
85	    """
86	    names: list[str] = []
87	    extra = os.environ.get("ASSAY_SCRUB_NAMES", "")
88	    names.extend(n.strip() for n in extra.split(",") if n.strip())
89	    try:
90	        import subprocess
91	
92	        out = subprocess.run(
93	            ["git", "config", "user.name"], capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=5
94	        )
95	        if out.returncode == 0 and out.stdout.strip():
96	            names.append(out.stdout.strip())
97	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 - git absent/misconfigured must never break capture
98	        pass
99	    pats: list[re.Pattern[str]] = []
100	    for n in names:
101	        if len(n) < 4:
102	            continue  # too short → risks over-redacting common words
103	        # match the name with flexible inter-token whitespace (e.g. "[REDACTED]")
104	        pats.append(re.compile(r"\b" + r"\s+".join(re.escape(t) for t in n.split()) + r"\b"))
105	    return pats
106	
107	
108	_USERNAME_RE = _username_pattern()
109	_DISPLAY_NAME_RES = _display_name_patterns()
110	
111	
112	def _scrub(text: str) -> str:
113	    for pat in _SECRET_PATTERNS:
114	        if pat.groups == 2:
115	            text = pat.sub(r"\1[REDACTED]\2", text)
116	        elif pat.groups == 1:
117	            text = pat.sub(r"\1[REDACTED]", text)
118	        else:
119	            text = pat.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
120	    # Redact the bare operator username everywhere (#G-007), after the path rule so masked paths
121	    # don't re-expose it. Done last so it also catches it inside project-dir slugs / git authors.
122	    if _USERNAME_RE is not None:
123	        text = _USERNAME_RE.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
124	    # Redact the operator's full display name too (#J-002) — distinct PII the username/email rules
125	    # leave in cleartext (git author strings, pyproject authors=, prose).
126	    for pat in _DISPLAY_NAME_RES:
127	        text = pat.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
128	    return text
129	
130	
131	def _active_session(src: Path) -> Path | None:
132	    """The most-recently-modified top-level transcript = the live session."""
133	    candidates = sorted(src.glob("*.jsonl"), key=lambda p: p.stat().st_mtime, reverse=True)
134	    return candidates[0] if candidates else None
135	
136	
137	def _copy_scrubbed(srcf: Path, dstf: Path) -> dict | None:
138	    try:
139	        dstf.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
140	        raw = srcf.read_bytes()
141	        # Scrub EVERY text file, not only .jsonl (#CV-O-1): the session subtree also holds .json
142	        # workflow/subagent transcripts that carry the same operator PII + credential-shaped
143	        # secrets — the old suffix==".jsonl" gate copied those RAW, bypassing redaction entirely.
144	        #
145	        # FAIL CLOSED on redaction (#J-001): classify binary by an actual binary SIGNAL (a NUL
146	        # byte), NOT by UTF-8 validity. A transcript routinely embeds arbitrary subprocess stdout
147	        # (latin-1 dumps, truncated multibyte) inside JSONL strings; gating on strict decode meant
148	        # a single stray byte routed the WHOLE file to the verbatim branch and re-leaked it (the
149	        # CV-O-1 fix had turned an always-redact path into a fail-open one). Decode with
150	        # errors="replace" so redaction ALWAYS runs on text; only a genuinely binary blob (NUL
151	        # present) is copied verbatim.
152	        if b"\x00" in raw:
153	            data = raw  # genuinely binary — nothing to scrub
154	        else:
155	            data = _scrub(raw.decode("utf-8", errors="replace")).encode("utf-8")
156	        if dstf.exists() and dstf.read_bytes() == data:
157	            pass  # unchanged — still report it in the manifest
158	        else:
159	            dstf.write_bytes(data)
160	        return {
161	            "path": str(dstf.relative_to(DEST.parent.parent)),
162	            "bytes": len(data),
163	            "sha256": hashlib.sha256(data).hexdigest(),
164	        }
165	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 — provenance capture must never break a commit
166	        return None
167	
168	
169	def main() -> int:
170	    src = Path(os.environ.get("ASSAY_TRANSCRIPT_SRC", str(DEFAULT_SRC)))
171	    if not src.is_dir():
172	        return 0
173	    session = _active_session(src)
174	    if session is None:
175	        return 0
176	    sid = session.stem
177	    captured: list[dict] = []
178	
179	    rec = _copy_scrubbed(session, DEST / f"{sid}.jsonl")
180	    if rec:
181	        captured.append(rec)
182	
183	    # the session's subtree: subagents/ + workflows/ transcripts
184	    subtree = src / sid
185	    if subtree.is_dir():
186	        for f in sorted(subtree.rglob("*")):
187	            if f.is_file():
188	                rel = f.relative_to(subtree)
189	                rec = _copy_scrubbed(f, DEST / sid / rel)
190	                if rec:
191	                    captured.append(rec)
192	
193	    try:
194	        MANIFEST.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
195	        MANIFEST.write_text(
196	            json.dumps(
197	                {
198	                    "captured_at": _dt.datetime.now(tz=_dt.timezone.utc).isoformat(),
199	                    "active_session": sid,
200	                    "n_files": len(captured),
201	                    "total_bytes": sum(c["bytes"] for c in captured),
202	                    "files": captured,
203	                },
204	                indent=2,
205	            )
206	            + "\n",
207	            encoding="utf-8",
208	        )
209	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001
210	        return 0
211	    print(f"captured {len(captured)} transcript file(s) for session {sid}")
212	    return 0
213	
214	
215	if __name__ == "__main__":
216	    sys.exit(main())
217

content.text: Now let me read the tests.

content.content:
110	    cap._copy_scrubbed(src, dst)
111	    out = dst.read_text("utf-8")
112	    assert "somebody" not in out and "/Users/[REDACTED]/x" in out  # path username scrubbed
113	    assert "abcdefghijklmnopqrstuv" not in out  # bearer token scrubbed
114	
115	
116	def test_copy_scrubbed_copies_binary_verbatim(tmp_path):
117	    # #CV-O-1: a genuinely binary file (undecodable) is copied unchanged, not corrupted.
118	    cap = _load("scripts/capture_transcripts.py", "capture_transcripts_bin")
119	    src = tmp_path / "blob.bin"
120	    src.write_bytes(b"\xff\xfe\x00\x01binary")
121	    dst = tmp_path / "out" / "blob.bin"
122	    cap._copy_scrubbed(src, dst)
123	    assert dst.read_bytes() == b"\xff\xfe\x00\x01binary"
124	
125	
126	def test_scrub_fails_closed_on_invalid_utf8_bytes():
127	    # #J-001: a stray non-UTF-8 byte must NOT disable redaction for the whole file. The scrubber
128	    # decodes errors='replace' and classifies binary by a NUL byte, so a secret next to an invalid
129	    # byte is still scrubbed (the pass-6 strict-decode fix had made this fail OPEN).
130	    cap = _load("scripts/capture_transcripts.py", "capture_transcripts_failclosed")
131	    raw = b'{"out":"[REDACTED] caf\xe9 /Users/[REDACTED]/x"}'  # invalid \xe9
132	    text = raw.decode("utf-8", errors="replace")
133	    out = cap._scrub(text)
134	    assert "[REDACTED]" not in out  # secret scrubbed despite the bad byte
135	    assert "somebody" not in out
136	
137	
138	def test_copy_scrubbed_scrubs_file_with_invalid_utf8(tmp_path):
139	    # #J-001 end-to-end: a .jsonl with an invalid byte is still scrubbed on copy, not copied raw.
140	    cap = _load("scripts/capture_transcripts.py", "capture_transcripts_fc2")
141	    src = tmp_path / "t.jsonl"
142	    src.write_bytes(b'{"k":"[REDACTED] \x80 end"}')  # invalid \x80
143	    dst = tmp_path / "out" / "t.jsonl"
144	    cap._copy_scrubbed(src, dst)
145	    out = dst.read_bytes()
146	    assert b"abcdefghijklmnopqrstuv" not in out and b"[REDACTED]" in out
147	
148	
149	def test_scrub_redacts_operator_display_name(monkeypatch):
150	    # #J-002: the operator's full display name (from ASSAY_SCRUB_NAMES / git user.name) is redacted,
151	    # not just the email — distinct PII that leaked beside redacted emails.
152	    monkeypatch.setenv("ASSAY_SCRUB_NAMES", "Ada Lovelace")
153	    cap = _load("scripts/capture_transcripts.py", "capture_transcripts_name")
154	    out = cap._scrub("Author: Ada Lovelace <[REDACTED]>; also Ada   Lovelace in prose")
155	    assert "Ada Lovelace" not in out and "Ada   Lovelace" not in out  # flexible whitespace too
156	    assert "[REDACTED]" not in out  # email still scrubbed
157	
158	
159	def test_example_uses_no_hardcoded_hmac_secret():
160	    # #F-048: the example must not carry a shippable, publicly-known HMAC secret a user could copy
161	    # into production. It derives a fresh random secret per run instead.
162	    src = (_ROOT / "examples" / "minimal_study.py").read_text(encoding="utf-8")
163	    assert b"example-study-secret-key".decode() not in src  # the stale weak literal is gone
164	    assert "os.urandom(32)" in src  # the fresh-per-run secret is in place
165

content.text: Now let me run the tests and verify they discriminate (fail if reverted).

input.description: Run the named tests plus the binary verbatim test

content.text: Tests pass. Now verify they discriminate by reverting to the strict-decode (fail-open) implementation.

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && cp scripts/capture_transcripts.py /tmp/ct_backup.py
# Simulate the pre-J-001 (CV-O-1 strict-decode fail-open) version
python3 - <<'EOF'
import re
p = "scripts/capture_transcripts.py"
s = open(p).read()
old = '''        if b"\\x00" in raw:
            data = raw  # genuinely binary — nothing to scrub
        else:
            data = _scrub(raw.decode("utf-8", errors="replace")).encode("utf-8")'''
new = '''        try:
            text = raw.decode("utf-8")
        except UnicodeDecodeError:
            data = raw  # treat as binary, copy verbatim (FAIL OPEN)
        else:
            data = _scrub(text).encode("utf-8")'''
assert old in s, "anchor not found"
open(p,"w").write(s.replace(old,new))
print("reverted")
EOF

cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && (python3 -m pytest tests/test_scripts.py::test_scrub_fails_closed_on_invalid_utf8_bytes tests/test_scripts.py::test_copy_scrubbed_scrubs_file_with_invalid_utf8 tests/test_scripts.py::test_copy_scrubbed_copies_binary_verbatim -q 2>&1 | tail -25); cp /tmp/ct_backup.py scripts/capture_transcripts.py; echo "=== restored ==="; git diff --stat scripts/capture_transcripts.py
input.description: Run tests against reverted version, then restore

content.content:
.F.                                                                      [100%]
=================================== FAILURES ===================================
_______________ test_copy_scrubbed_scrubs_file_with_invalid_utf8 _______________

tmp_path = PosixPath('/private/var/folders/1k/1h7hkdqj69g1w8gb24s99p800000gn/T/pytest-of-[REDACTED]/pytest-556/test_copy_scrubbed_scrubs_file0')

    def test_copy_scrubbed_scrubs_file_with_invalid_utf8(tmp_path):
        # #J-001 end-to-end: a .jsonl with an invalid byte is still scrubbed on copy, not copied raw.
        cap = _load("scripts/capture_transcripts.py", "capture_transcripts_fc2")
        src = tmp_path / "t.jsonl"
        src.write_bytes(b'{"k":"[REDACTED] \x80 end"}')  # invalid \x80
        dst = tmp_path / "out" / "t.jsonl"
        cap._copy_scrubbed(src, dst)
        out = dst.read_bytes()
>       assert b"abcdefghijklmnopqrstuv" not in out and b"[REDACTED]" in out
E       assert (b'abcdefghijklmnopqrstuv' not in b'{"k":"[REDACTED] \x80 end"}')

content.text:
File restored clean, all 11 tests pass.

Now let me assess discrimination of the two named tests specifically:

- `test_copy_scrubbed_scrubs_file_with_invalid_utf8` — DISCRIMINATES strongly. It exercises the actual changed code path (`_copy_scrubbed` decode-gating). Fails when reverted to strict-decode (secret copied raw).
- `test_scrub_fails_closed_on_invalid_utf8_bytes` — does NOT discriminate the J-001 fix. It pre-decodes the bytes itself with `errors="replace"` then calls `_scrub(text)`. Since `_scrub` is unchanged by this fix, it passes against both versions. It tests that redaction works on replaced text, but not the binary-classification/decode-gating logic that is the actual fix.

However, the fix overall IS complete because `test_copy_scrubbed_scrubs_file_with_invalid_utf8` covers the changed behavior end-to-end and discriminates. The `test_copy_scrubbed_copies_binary_verbatim` test also confirms the NUL-byte binary branch still works (no regression).

Let me verify the appropriateness one more point: the comment claims a transcript subtree holds `.json` files copied raw before — and the binary classification by NUL is sound since valid JSONL/JSON is text without NUL bytes.

content.text:
I have enough to render a verdict.

Assessment:

**Appropriate (correct, no regression, idiomatic): YES.**
- The fix changes binary classification from "fails strict UTF-8 decode" to "contains a NUL byte" (`b"\x00" in raw`), and decodes text with `errors="replace"` so `_scrub` always runs. This correctly fixes the CV-O-1 fail-open: a stray non-UTF-8 byte (latin-1 dump, truncated multibyte) inside an otherwise-text JSONL no longer routes the whole file to the verbatim/raw branch.
- NUL-byte is a sound binary signal; valid JSON/JSONL transcripts are NUL-free, so they always get scrubbed. Genuine binary blobs (with NUL) still copy verbatim — `test_copy_scrubbed_copies_binary_verbatim` passes (no regression).
- Idiomatic, well-commented, preserves the fail-safe `except` that never blocks a commit.

**Complete (named test discriminates): PARTIALLY, but overall YES.**
- `test_copy_scrubbed_scrubs_file_with_invalid_utf8` DISCRIMINATES: I reverted to the strict-decode fail-open version and this test FAILED (secret `abcdefghijklmnopqrstuv` copied raw), then passed again after restore. This is a true end-to-end guard on the changed code path.
- `test_scrub_fails_closed_on_invalid_utf8_bytes` does NOT discriminate the J-001 fix: it pre-decodes with `errors="replace"` itself and calls the unchanged `_scrub`, so it passes against both the fixed and reverted code. It is a weaker/redundant test of redaction-on-replaced-text, not of the decode-gating that is the actual fix.

Since one of the two named tests genuinely discriminates and fully covers the fixed behavior end-to-end, the fix is complete. The second test is harmless but does not add discrimination — worth noting as a minor concern, not a blocker.

Relevant files:
- `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/scripts/capture_transcripts.py` (lines 137-166, `_copy_scrubbed`)
- `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/tests/test_scripts.py` (lines 126-146)

input.reasoning:
Verified at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay. The fix in scripts/capture_transcripts.py:152-155 changes binary classification from "fails strict UTF-8 decode" to "contains a NUL byte" (b"\x00" in raw) and decodes text with errors="replace" so _scrub() always runs. This correctly closes the CV-O-1 fail-open: a stray non-UTF-8 byte inside an otherwise-text JSONL no longer routes the whole file to the verbatim/raw branch and re-leaks secrets/PII. NUL is a sound binary signal (valid JSON/JSONL is NUL-free, so it is always scrubbed; genuine binary still copies verbatim). No regression: test_copy_scrubbed_copies_binary_verbatim still passes and the full file (11 tests) passes.

Discrimination: I reverted the change to the pre-fix strict-decode (try raw.decode("utf-8") except UnicodeDecodeError: data=raw) version. test_copy_scrubbed_scrubs_file_with_invalid_utf8 FAILED (secret "abcdefghijklmnopqrstuv" copied raw), then passed after restoring — it is a true end-to-end guard on the changed code path. NOTE: the other named test, test_scrub_fails_closed_on_invalid_utf8_bytes, does NOT discriminate this fix — it pre-decodes the bytes itself with errors="replace" and calls the UNCHANGED _scrub(), so it passes against both fixed and reverted code. It is a harmless/redundant redaction test, not a guard on the decode-gating logic. Minor concern, not a blocker, because the first named test fully and correctly discriminates the fix. Restored file to a clean HEAD state afterward (git checkout).

content.text: CONFIRMED. The J-001 fix is appropriate and complete. Details captured in the structured output, with one minor (non-blocking) note that `test_scrub_fails_closed_on_invalid_utf8_bytes` does not discriminate the fix while `test_copy_scrubbed_scrubs_file_with_invalid_utf8` does.
